hero-start.title: It's Turtles All the Way Down
hero-start.subtitle: A deck about a tool that builds decks. Built with the tool.

the-premise.title: The Recursion Is the Point
the-premise.body:
This presentation is a deck spec — a JSON graph of nodes and edges, rendered by a single HTML file with zero dependencies.

That same engine generated this spec. You are currently inside the output of the tool the tool was built to produce.

The turtle is standing on a turtle.
bullets: Single index.html — no build step, no framework
Decks are JSON: nodes, types, edges
CLI wraps Claude to generate specs from any source
Branching lets audiences choose their own path

turtle-branch.title: Which turtle are you?
turtle-branch.subtitle: Choose your depth. All paths converge.

branches.label: The Philosophy
branches.desc: I want to understand what makes this recursive and why it matters

branches.label: The Mechanics
branches.desc: Show me the JSON spec format and how the engine actually works

branches.label: The CLI
branches.desc: Skip the theory — just show me the commands to generate a deck

meta-turtle.title: The Turtle Holding Up the Turtle
meta-turtle.body:
The classic cosmological problem: what does the world rest on? A turtle. And that turtle? Another turtle.

Deck-engine has the same structure. A deck spec describes how to present information about deck specs. The tool that renders this was described by a spec that was generated by a tool that generates specs.

There is no bottom turtle. That's not a bug — it's the whole point. When a medium can describe itself, you've built something with real expressive power.
bullets: The spec format is simple enough to write by hand
Claude can generate a spec from any source — including this one
You can refine a deck by talking to the CLI
The output is always a file you own and can inspect

practical-turtle.title: The Spec Is the Whole Thing
practical-turtle.body:
A deck is a JSON graph. Nodes have types: hero, content, branch. Edges are next (linear) or branches[] (choice).

The renderer walks the graph, builds DOM from node data, and manages a history stack for back-navigation. CSS custom properties handle all theming.
practical-turtle.code: // Every node looks like this "my-node": { "type": "content", "title": "A Slide", "bullets": ["Point one", "Point two"], "next": "next-node" }

cli-turtle.title: Three Commands, One Turtle
cli-turtle.body: The deck CLI is a bash script wrapping claude -p. Point it at any source — a README, a repo URL, a wall of notes — and it outputs a valid deck spec.
cli-turtle.code: # Generate from any source ./deck generate README.md --audience mixed --goal demo # Refine with plain English ./deck refine deck.json "add a branch for skeptics" # Serve it instantly ./deck serve deck.json

the-demo.title: You Are the Demo
the-demo.body:
You just navigated a branching deck about a tool that builds branching decks. The demo has been running this whole time.

Every path you didn't take still exists in the spec. Hit A to open the analytics overlay and see exactly which nodes you visited, which branches you skipped, and how long you spent on each slide.

Then run ./deck generate on something you actually want to present. The turtle stack continues.
bullets: Press A — analytics overlay shows your path through the graph
Press Esc — return to landing, load a different JSON deck
Drag any .json deck file onto the landing page to load it
Pass ?deck=myfile.json as a URL param to share a specific deck

hero-close.title: Go Build Something Recursive
hero-close.subtitle: The turtle at the bottom is the one you make next.
